files./src/main.jsx:
import React from 'react'
import { createRoot } from 'react-dom/client'
import App from './App.jsx'
createRoot(document.getElementById('root')).render(<App />)

scenes.narration: The **useState** hook lets a component remember a value between renders. We'll build a counter.
scenes.say: The useState hook lets a component remember a value between renders. Let's build a counter, and explain every line.
First we import useState from React. It is a named export, so it goes in curly braces.

scenes.narration: `App` is a function component that returns JSX.
scenes.say: Our component is a function called App. Whatever it returns is what React draws.

scenes.narration: `useState(0)` returns `[value, setter]` — here, `count` and `setCount`, starting at 0.
scenes.say: useState of zero is the key line. It returns a pair: the current value, count, and a function to update it, setCount. We name both with array destructuring, and the starting value is zero. Every time setCount runs, React re-renders with the new value.
Now the markup the component returns.

A wrapping div styled with Tailwind. min height screen makes it at least the viewport height. b g slate fifty is a light grey background. p eight is padding on all sides. And text slate nine hundred is dark text.
A heading. text two x l for size, font bold for weight.

scenes.narration: The button reads `count` and updates it with `setCount`.
scenes.say: The button. m t four adds top margin. rounded l g gives rounded corners. b g indigo six hundred is the background color, with text white for the label. p x four and p y two are horizontal and vertical padding. hover b g indigo five hundred lightens it on hover. And the onClick calls setCount with count plus one, wrapped in an arrow function so it only runs on click.

action.type:
<button
        className="mt-4 rounded-lg bg-indigo-600 px-4 py-2 text-white hover:bg-indigo-500"
        onClick={() => setCount(count + 1)}
      >
        count is {count}
      </button>

scenes.narration: Drop the arrow function and `setCount` runs on **every render** — an infinite loop.
scenes.say: Here's the classic mistake. If you drop the arrow function and call setCount directly, React runs it during every render. That triggers another render, which calls it again — an infinite loop. React throws too many re-renders.

diagnostics.message: Calling setCount during render causes 'Too many re-renders'. Wrap it in an arrow function.

scenes.narration: Wrap it in an arrow function — it only fires **on click**.
scenes.say: The fix is the arrow function. Now setCount only runs when the user actually clicks.

scenes.narration: Here it is running live, beside the code.
scenes.say: And here it is running live, right beside the code.

scenes.narration: Each click calls `setCount`; React re-renders with the new count.
scenes.say: Watch the counter. Each click calls setCount, React re-renders, and the number goes up. That is useState.
